past_medical_history: Epilepsy diagnosed at age 14 after a witnessed focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure; treated with topiramate 100 mg/day for the past 17 years with partial seizure control
History of febrile seizure at age 2
Mild iron deficiency anemia during pregnancy, resolved with supplementation
family_history: Mother with epilepsy, controlled on carbamazepine
Maternal grandmother with history of recurrent seizures
One sibling (brother) with childhood absence epilepsy, now in remission

social_history.occupation: Elementary school teacher, currently on maternity leave
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and 6-week-old daughter in an apartment
social_history.driving: Has not been driving due to seizure recurrence in the past year; license suspended per state law
social_history.breastfeeding: Currently breastfeeding with formula supplementation; two daily breast-feedings replaced with formula one week after birth

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech fluent without paraphasic errors. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Recall 3/3 at five minutes. Mildly anxious but cooperative. Post-ictal examination performed approximately 4 hours after the witnessed seizure; patient has returned to baseline.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally (3 mm to 2 mm). Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally in all three divisions of CN V. Facial movements symmetric. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout. Strength 5/5 in all extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift. No involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, proprioception, and vibration intact in all extremities. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing without dysmetria bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Small healing lateral tongue bite on the right side, consistent with seizure activity. No other focal neurological deficits.

patient.chief_complaint: Witnessed generalized seizure with preceding aura of an intense smell of burnt rubber and a rising sensation from the stomach, occurring despite being on topiramate for several years
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Ramirez is a 31-year-old right-handed woman with a long-standing history of epilepsy diagnosed at age 14 who presents to the emergency department after a witnessed focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure at home. She has been maintained on topiramate 100 mg daily for approximately 17 years with partial seizure control, experiencing on average two to three focal seizures per year characterized by brief olfactory hallucinations described as a strong smell of burnt rubber followed by an epigastric rising sensation. These aura episodes typically last 20 to 40 seconds and resolve spontaneously without loss of awareness. She delivered her first child six weeks ago via uncomplicated vaginal delivery and has been breastfeeding with partial formula supplementation. Her husband reports that today, approximately four hours prior to arrival, the patient was seated on the couch while holding the infant when she suddenly called out that she was smelling burnt rubber. He immediately took the infant from her arms. She then stared blankly ahead and began making repetitive lip-smacking and swallowing movements for approximately 15 seconds. Her right hand began rhythmically picking at her clothing. She then developed bilateral arm stiffening followed by generalized rhythmic jerking of all four extremities lasting approximately 70 seconds. She was incontinent of urine during the event. Post-ictally, she was confused and drowsy for approximately 25 minutes before gradually becoming oriented again. The patient reports that her seizure frequency had been slowly increasing over the past several months, particularly since the third trimester of pregnancy, and she has had approximately five aura episodes in the past month alone compared to her baseline of two to three per year. She attributes this increase to sleep deprivation associated with caring for a newborn, though she continued her topiramate unchanged throughout pregnancy and postpartum. She denies any recent medication changes, missed doses, illness, fever, or head trauma. No other family members have been ill. She reports persistent fatigue and occasional headaches attributed to sleep deprivation but denies any new neurological symptoms.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz, symmetric, well-modulated alpha rhythm attenuating appropriately with eye opening
background.sleep_features: Vertex waves and sleep spindles observed during brief periods of drowsiness and stage II sleep

findings.frequency: Frequent, occurring every 1-2 minutes during wakefulness and increasing during drowsiness and early sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8 with field extending to T4 and F4, duration 50-80 ms, followed by after-going slow wave
findings.state: Wakefulness, drowsiness, and NREM sleep; maximal during drowsiness

findings.frequency: Intermittent runs of 2-3 Hz rhythmic delta activity lasting 4-10 seconds
findings.state: Predominantly during drowsiness and light sleep

artifacts.impact: Minimal impact on interpretation; interictal discharges clearly identified

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes performed; no provocation of electrographic seizure activity. Mild symmetric background slowing noted.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at 10-18 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response elicited.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG due to frequent right anterior temporal sharp waves with phase reversal at F8 and intermittent right temporal rhythmic delta activity (TIRDA). No electrographic seizures were captured during this 45-minute routine recording. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute recording without ictal events captured. Prolonged video-EEG monitoring recommended to capture habitual seizures and correlate with clinical semiology.

findings.size: Right hippocampal volume reduced by approximately 30% compared to the left

signal_characteristics.T1: Decreased volume and loss of internal architecture of the right hippocampus
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in the right hippocampus compared to the left
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal in the right hippocampus, most pronounced in the body

findings.borders: Well-defined volume loss without associated mass lesion

volumetrics.right_hippocampus: 2.0 cm3 (below 5th percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: No other cortical or subcortical signal abnormalities identified
Ventricles and sulci normal in caliber for patient age
No abnormal enhancement following gadolinium administration
mri.impression: Right mesial temporal sclerosis with significant hippocampal volume loss and T2/FLAIR hyperintensity. No mass lesion, acute infarction, or hemorrhage. Findings are concordant with a right temporal lobe epileptogenic focus. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm. No arrhythmia, conduction abnormality, or ischemic changes detected.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Bicarbonate 21 mEq/L (L), Prolactin 48.2 ng/mL (H), Topiramate level 3.2 mcg/mL (L).

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9 Hz alpha, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture recorded over 48 hours of continuous monitoring

findings.frequency: Frequent, averaging 15 per hour during wakefulness, increasing to 30 per hour during NREM sleep
findings.morphology: Phase reversal at F8, maximum negativity at F8/T4, followed by after-going slow wave
findings.state: All states of consciousness, maximal during NREM sleep

findings.location: Right anterior temporal onset (F8/T4), evolving to right hemispheric
findings.frequency: One clinical seizure captured over 48 hours
findings.morphology: Seizure onset with right temporal rhythmic 5-6 Hz theta activity evolving to 2-3 Hz delta with spread to right parasagittal region over 45 seconds
findings.state: Occurred during wakefulness while the patient was speaking with nursing staff

findings.morphology: Rhythmic 2-3 Hz delta activity in right temporal chain

artifacts.location: Right temporal leads, during oral automatisms
artifacts.impact: Mild, did not obscure seizure onset

output.impression: Abnormal 48-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. One habitual clinical seizure captured with clear right anterior temporal electrographic onset (F8/T4), concordant with clinical semiology of a temporal lobe seizure (olfactory aura, epigastric rising, behavioral arrest, oral automatisms). Frequent right anterior temporal interictal epileptiform discharges. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Only one seizure captured. Additional monitoring may be needed for surgical evaluation.

findings.location: Right hippocampus, most prominent in the body segment
findings.size: Right hippocampal volume 1.9 cm3 versus left 3.4 cm3, asymmetry ratio 0.28

signal_characteristics.T1_3D: Loss of normal internal structure of the right hippocampus with reduced gray-white differentiation
signal_characteristics.T2_coronal: Markedly hyperintense signal in the right hippocampus on thin coronal cuts (3 mm slices perpendicular to the long axis of the hippocampus)
signal_characteristics.FLAIR_coronal: Hyperintense signal in the right hippocampal body, clearly abnormal compared to the left

findings.mass_effect: None. Mild ex vacuo dilation of the right temporal horn.
findings.borders: Well-defined atrophic changes without associated mass

signal_characteristics.overall: Normal signal throughout the cerebral cortex, white matter, basal ganglia, thalami, brainstem, and cerebellum

additional_observations: No evidence of focal cortical dysplasia
output.impression: No dual pathology or other structural abnormalities identified. Findings concordant with right temporal lobe epilepsy. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Visual Memory (BVMT-R Total Learning) T-score 36 T-score (H), Visual Memory (BVMT-R Delayed Recall) T-score 32 T-score (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Visual memory consolidation (BVMT-R Delayed Recall) T-score 32

findings.location: Right mesial and lateral temporal lobe

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Markedly reduced FDG uptake in the right mesial temporal structures (hippocampus, parahippocampal gyrus, and amygdala) and moderately reduced uptake in the right lateral temporal neocortex compared to the left
signal_characteristics.asymmetry_index: Right-to-left temporal lobe asymmetry index 0.76 (normal >0.90), indicating significant right temporal hypometabolism

findings.location: Bilateral frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes; left temporal lobe; basal ganglia; thalami; cerebellum

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Symmetric and normal FDG uptake throughout the remainder of the brain

additional_observations: Interictal FDG-PET acquired during EEG-confirmed interictal state
No areas of hypermetabolism to suggest ictal activity during acquisition
output.impression: Interictal FDG-PET demonstrates significant right mesial and lateral temporal hypometabolism concordant with right mesial temporal sclerosis on MRI and right temporal epileptiform activity on EEG. Multi-modal concordance strongly supports right temporal lobe epilepsy and is favorable for surgical candidacy. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Topiramate serum level 3.2 mcg/mL (L).

output.query: topiramate breastfeeding safety epilepsy postpartum seizure management

results.title: Antiepileptic drug use in breastfeeding mothers: a systematic review
results.summary: Topiramate is excreted in breast milk at relatively high concentrations (milk-to-plasma ratio ~0.86). Infant exposure is estimated at 3-23% of the maternal weight-adjusted dose. Gastrointestinal side effects including diarrhea have been reported in breastfed infants exposed to topiramate.

results.title: Postpartum seizure control and AED pharmacokinetics
results.summary: AED clearance changes significantly during pregnancy and postpartum. Topiramate clearance increases by 30-40% during pregnancy and returns to baseline over 2-4 weeks postpartum. Therapeutic drug monitoring is recommended during the perinatal period.

results.title: ILAE/AAN practice guideline: management of epilepsy in women of childbearing age
results.summary: Lamotrigine and levetiracetam are considered compatible with breastfeeding with relatively low infant exposure. Dose adjustments should be guided by therapeutic drug monitoring during the postpartum period.

output.summary: Topiramate crosses into breast milk at significant concentrations and has been associated with gastrointestinal adverse effects in exposed infants. Postpartum pharmacokinetic changes can lead to subtherapeutic AED levels and seizure breakthrough. Lamotrigine and levetiracetam are generally considered the safest AEDs for breastfeeding mothers. Therapeutic drug monitoring is essential during the perinatal period to maintain seizure control.

output.proposed: Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily (titrate to 1000 mg twice daily)
interactions: No significant interactions with topiramate (if cross-titrating)
No significant interactions with prenatal vitamins
warnings: Psychiatric adverse effects (irritability, mood changes) in approximately 10-15% of patients
Low excretion in breast milk; generally considered compatible with breastfeeding
Dose adjustment needed in renal impairment (not applicable with normal creatinine 0.72 mg/dL)
output.formulary_status: On formulary, no prior authorization required
alternatives: Lamotrigine 25 mg daily, titrate slowly to 200 mg daily (favorable breastfeeding profile, but requires slow titration)
Oxcarbazepine 300 mg twice daily, titrate to 600 mg twice daily (higher breast milk excretion)
Lacosamide 50 mg twice daily, titrate to 200 mg twice daily (limited breastfeeding data)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy - temporal lobe (right mesial temporal lobe epilepsy with hippocampal sclerosis)

differential.key_features: Witnessed semiology is classic for temporal lobe seizure with stereotyped olfactory aura, automatisms, and secondary generalization. Elevated post-ictal prolactin and clear EEG abnormalities strongly argue against PNES.
No prodrome of lightheadedness. Prolonged post-ictal confusion inconsistent with syncope. Normal ECG. Tonic-clonic activity lasting 70 seconds far exceeds brief convulsive syncope.

Migraine aura spreads slowly over 5-20 minutes and is typically visual or sensory. Patient's episodes are brief (seconds), include olfactory hallucinations and epigastric rising (classic temporal lobe aura), and are followed by altered awareness and automatisms.
Patient is 31 years old with no vascular risk factors. TIAs produce negative symptoms (weakness, numbness), not positive phenomena like olfactory hallucinations and automatisms.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed seizure history including aura description, semiology, witness account, and post-ictal period
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Classic temporal lobe aura (olfactory hallucination, epigastric rising) followed by focal to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure with post-ictal confusion
Normal interictal exam with lateral tongue bite consistent with seizure
Right anterior temporal sharp waves and TIRDA consistent with right temporal epileptogenic focus

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain with epilepsy protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right hippocampal sclerosis with volume loss and T2/FLAIR hyperintensity

optimal_actions.action: Obtain labs including BMP, CBC, prolactin, AED level, and urine drug screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic panel except mildly low bicarbonate, elevated post-ictal prolactin, subtherapeutic topiramate level, negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to rule out cardiac etiology
Address subtherapeutic topiramate level and optimize AED therapy; consider switching to levetiracetam or lamotrigine given breastfeeding
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant drug interactions; levetiracetam and lamotrigine compatible with breastfeeding

optimal_actions.action: Counsel patient on seizure safety precautions including not holding infant alone during feedings and continued driving restriction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient counseled on safety measures for caring for infant with active epilepsy

optimal_actions.action: Refer to comprehensive epilepsy center for surgical evaluation given structural lesion and medically refractory seizures
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Referral placed for epilepsy surgery workup

critical_actions: Obtain EEG to characterize epileptiform activity and lateralize the epileptogenic focus
Obtain brain MRI to confirm structural etiology (hippocampal sclerosis)
Obtain basic metabolic panel and CBC to exclude metabolic causes of seizure breakthrough
Check AED serum levels to assess for subtherapeutic drug concentration as a cause of breakthrough seizures
Optimize antiepileptic drug therapy considering breastfeeding safety
Counsel patient on seizure safety precautions when caring for a newborn
contraindicated_actions: Performing lumbar puncture in this clinical scenario (no indication for CSF analysis; no signs of infection or inflammation)
Prescribing valproic acid due to significant teratogenicity risk in a woman of childbearing age and high breast milk excretion
Discharging patient without addressing subtherapeutic AED levels and seizure breakthrough
Prescribing bupropion which lowers seizure threshold
Advising the patient that she can resume driving immediately
Abruptly discontinuing topiramate without cross-titrating to an alternative AED (risk of withdrawal seizures)
key_reasoning_points: Olfactory hallucinations (burnt rubber smell) combined with epigastric rising sensation is a classic mesial temporal lobe aura, strongly localizing seizure onset to the uncinate region of the temporal lobe
The stereotyped nature of the aura episodes over years confirms these are focal aware seizures, and the patient's known epilepsy is consistent with temporal lobe origin
History of febrile seizures in childhood is a well-established risk factor for mesial temporal sclerosis and subsequent temporal lobe epilepsy

Subtherapeutic topiramate level (3.2 mcg/mL) in the postpartum period is a key modifiable factor explaining the increase in seizure frequency; postpartum pharmacokinetic changes significantly affect AED metabolism
Concordance across modalities (semiology with olfactory aura, right temporal EEG abnormalities, right hippocampal sclerosis on MRI) confirms right temporal lobe epilepsy

AED choice must account for breastfeeding safety; levetiracetam and lamotrigine have the most favorable profiles for breastfeeding mothers compared to topiramate which has significant breast milk excretion
Mildly low bicarbonate is consistent with topiramate's known carbonic anhydrase inhibitory effect and is not indicative of an alternative diagnosis

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward - classic temporal lobe epilepsy presentation with stereotyped olfactory aura, clear MRI lesion (hippocampal sclerosis), and concordant EEG findings. Subtherapeutic AED level provides a clear explanation for seizure breakthrough.
